speaker: interviewer
text: 29th of June, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, John, you're telling me you were in Wieliczka, basically, you and your family were there of your own free will. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said before, you were in Wieliczka for about a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after a year in Wieliczka? Your time in Wieliczka, I understand, was basically pleasant, given the consideration of the times that were around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, and I still went to the cheder over there, and my-- and we still did the normal, like the-- my mother and my sister were attending at home to doing the cooking and everything. I went to the cheder, and my father used to do a bit of business in Wieliczka and a bit of business at Cracow, coming there and back. And then, after, for some reason, we had to go back to-- to Cracow, to the ghetto. All the Jews had to go to ghetto, and we went, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a directive by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. But maybe we did not wait too long, because when we came to the, to Cracow, we had quite a good accommodation. We, we managed to get a, a good accommodation in Cracow. It was not more than two, two rooms, but it was sufficient for us. And we stayed in Cracow-- well, also, in the ghetto, we're also comfortable. There was no problem, as far as food or something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you arrived back in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I put it-- it could be, it could be about six or eight months before the liquidation. The liquidation was in March, '43, correct? We were there before, and when did Emalia start? The real Emalia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't remember it, because it would be about six, eight, nine months ago when I first start to go to Emalia with the first 60 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you said before you arrived back in Cracow around early '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Could be '42. And that was the whole '42. Not quite '42. Not quite, but let's say until October '42. In 1942, I start with Emalia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by that time, my father and my mother and my sister and her child were taken away. It, it, it wasn't-- they didn't wait until the liquidation of the ghetto. They were on the list. The Kapo came, and they were on the list, and they had to go. I wasn't on the list, because I was young, and my brother wasn't on the list. And, and my sister was not on the list, too, but she, but she didn't want to leave my parents, and she didn't want-- she was worried about the child. The child was only about four years or something like that. So she went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, assuming you arrived back in Cracow, you went straight into the ghetto. You had a two-bedroom or two-room accommodation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food, what were you able to do with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to buy food, and I used to go from time to time, being the youngest, used to go to-- because it was across the road-- to the neighbors, and I used to buy from them or get from them the food. I was caught once. That, I remembered very well. I was caught once by a Polish policeman. I had bread with me, a whole bread, and I was caught by the Polish policeman. And he said to me, you're a Jew. Because I didn't wear the-- because I, I couldn't be-- so I said, I'm not a Jew. So he said, come to the-- come, come into the-- you know, they used to have a big-- before you went into the block of flats, there was a, a big door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said come behind the door and take off your trousers. So I didn't went so far. I said to him, I am a Jew, and I start to cry, and I tell him, look, I only came here because we haven't got much bread, and I want to go back. So he said to me, I'll let you out this time. And this was the truth. He let me out. But if I ever catch you again, you're gone. And that was-- and I never went, since then, because I was scared that he would be there, and he would recognize me. It was the only accident which I will never forget. How old were you with this incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 15, maybe, 15 or 15 and 1/2, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was illegal to leave the ghetto, basically?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was illegal to get in. It was illegal to smuggle the food into the ghetto. But, you know, I did a few times, and once I was caught, and that's it. And I wasn't caught by the German. I was caught by the Polish policeman. But because-- I believe, because I didn't went as far as took off my trousers, because if I would have took my trousers, they knew that I was circumcised. Because the Polish people were not circumcised before the war. So I didn't went this far. I said, look, I am a Jew. And I told him the whole story, and I plead with him, and so he, he let me out. He let me have the bread, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the only story which I didn't tell you, but when we came to it, I must tell you. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the ghetto, what would people do for food? How were they able to obtain food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a kitchen where they used to get-- everybody used to get soup. The soup was quite [INAUDIBLE]. But besides, there was some shops in ghetto. You could obtain food and other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were businesses allowed to operate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so a few businesses were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Legally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Legally, yes, because it was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where would they get their supplies from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They smuggled, probably. They had some of it, you know. I wouldn't know. I wasn't, I wasn't that kind of-- but I know one thing, that you could get it. You had to have money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about-- how would you buy? How would you pay for these goods?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Polish Zloty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could use proper currency?

speaker: interviewee
text: Proper Polish currency, yes. And this currency could be converted from gold, from gold. You could convert into that currency from jewelry, you could convert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you convert gold into Zloty?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who buy gold, who buy the jewelry, who buy diamonds, you know? You could convert it. There was a value for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family were able to convert, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had, we had, we had plenty. As a matter of fact, I don't know whether I should say on television, but I still got hidden jewelry in Poland. And I went to the Polish, to the Polish consul here, and I asked them, I asked them whether I could go, if they assist me to take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he say to me that, if it is in the ground, it is the property of the government. I said, it's not in the ground. It's in the wall. He said to me, how far is from the ground? So I could see that I would have difficulty. So I leave it there. I know where it is, and it is still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what the value today, but it would be gold, jewelery, diamonds, and, and, and what you call it-- and a golden watch, which we convert it all. Because we had it just [INAUDIBLE] a little bit before the German came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, when-- this is 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your parents and-- when were they taken away? Do you remember the actual circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken away, they were taken just about a month before Emalia. Let's say about August, August or July in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Not '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. '43 was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the circumstances how they were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They, they came with a list, the, the Jewish Kapo. You know, they were Jewish militia, actually. They were dressed like a policeman, Jewish policeman. They had a star on the, on the cap. As they came with the list, they said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], we have to take you away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to your apartment, where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning. Very early in the morning. It could be before the dawn. And the whole ghetto was surrounded by the, by, by the German soldiers. Whenever there was action like that, there was fence that you couldn't escape. After they took a certain amount of people, there was no more guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is just before the liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before. This would be about six or nine months liquidation. Because they couldn't liquidate the whole ghetto at one go, so they liquidated gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm asking this, and you say it was the Jewish militia that came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, there was no Jewish-- the German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they, they had to go to a certain point, you know, to a-- what you call it-- where they all worked together. And they were sent by, by, by the trucks to the station, and to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage would have been collected on this particular morning? Was it a large section of the Jewish ghetto, or a small section?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, it was-- well, I don't know. They had a certain quota, but I wouldn't know how much it is. And also, a lot of people knew before that, because they had contact with the militia. Because militia got, got all this about the night before. And if they contact their parents and say, these people were safe, because they hide themselves. We didn't have that connection. We didn't have such a good connection. If we would have such a connection, probably my father would go underground, or somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many of these sort of actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were quite a few actions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they came and collect--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I only experience one, because that's where I experience when that happened to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was August '42, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But being in the ghetto, would you have known of other actions similar to this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was also other action, but at that particular time, we were not on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I understand, but you would have seen or heard what was going  on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it. I heard it, but I didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would have been, probably, six months before the liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Could be, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't a month before the liquidation. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I wasn't there when the liquidation was there, because I went to Emalia, as I told you, and we used to go from, from the ghetto to Emalia. We used to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we get there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you living at-- were you living with your parents when they were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK. Now let's go onto this Emalia. This was a factory in Cracow, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Oskar Schindler we're talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you were able to become involved in this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: By coincidence. I walk on the street, and I, I met a friend, and, and, and there was people standing, not in a queue, but in two rows. And there was a German over there, and there was a, a fellow which I knew, Bankier, which he was there. So I ask him, what's going on here? He said to me, they want plumbers for Emalia factory. He said it's supposed to be a good position, because you work inside. And I said, but you're not a plumber. I said, all right, I will try it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, and I, I joined the queue, joined the row. And when I joined the row, when the German came to me, he said to me, are you a plumber? I said, look, my parents are plumbers, you know. I come from a family of plumbers. So he took a risk, and he put me on that side where, where the 60 people is, and that's it. And I went to the factory. And the, and the next day-- I can't remember whether it was next day or the same day-- I went to the Emalia factory, and they put me-- I was, I was practically the youngest from the whole lot. And, and, and they put me in charge of a Polish foreman. So the Polish foreman came to me, what can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I can't do anything, I just want to come here, because I heard it would help me. So he said, all right, you, you have, you have a job. Here, take the broom and sweep all the floor. And that's how I started. And he taught me slowly how to, how to-- because it wasn't-- you didn't have to be skillful. All you had to do is to put a piece of metal under this special machine, and the machine went down, and the part come out. So it wasn't a job which you had to learn, go to the university. You could learn in five minutes. And that's how I entered Emalia. And I was there-- after that, I went to work outside. And then after, at the end of the Emalia, I used to work where I attend where there was the heating system for the whole factory. I had to pull cord, and I was to work at nighttime. This was the job. This was my job in Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a name, Bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bankier. Bankier was the old man who, who, who was-- he was the owner, before the war, of Emalia factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, wait a minute. Emalia factory, was that-- is that the official name of the factory, or did it have another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was Emalia. It was-- before the war, the Polish name Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emalia. E-M-L-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: E-M--

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-A. Why, why it was called Emalia, because they used to make pots, like Emalia, you know. They put them in the oven, and they used to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Enamel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enamel. That's right. That's how it was. So they called Emalia. And he went bankrupt before the war, with his other two partners, and Oskar Schindler took over the factory, but he needed somebody who knew something of the business part. Bankier was the one who looked after it. Stern, I never come in touch with Stern, but I knew Bankier. And Bankier--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's Stern?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stern which is in the Oskar-- the The accountant. He might be dead, but I can't remember it. I only remember Bankier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying you remember this Bankier. What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Bankier, I think. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Bankier? Or is it different on the list?

speaker: interviewer
text: Abraham. We'll have a look at the list after.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll have to look at the list, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're saying this Bankier, from your opinion, was more important to Schindler than any other person?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the most important man for Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say that? How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I know? Because he's the one who did, who did direct him to get the factory, and he's the one who run for him the factory, from the office. Because he knew the business. He knew the Emalia business. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Bankier--

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler didn't know anything about pots and pans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Bankier know anything about working in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. He knew it, because this factory was established before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were working, you said he was, he was part of the first 60 as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work on the floor with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Always-- as soon as we came to Emalia, to Emalia, when we enter it, we went to different ways, and he went to the office. I never saw him until the evening, when we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't involved with the physical work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't involved any physical work. He eat and drink-- he didn't eat with us. Nothing. He probably had special food, and all these things. And, and he was the one who, who, ran the Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or maybe he was an important person, and he needed to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And needed to be in the office to be, like, the administrator.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right. He was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relations, relationship with him? Did you know him? Were you able to speak to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know him from before the war, because he used to live in that, in that place in [NON-ENGLISH] where my sister was living, in the same building. I knew him before, and he knew me too. But-- and, and when I went to Emalia, he didn't, he didn't want to make [INAUDIBLE] that he knows me, or that there's some, some connection, but, but he didn't do me any harm. He helped me in many ways, like, for example, I don't know whether he wasn't the one who, who did try to get me on the list, or something, when later came, you know. So indirectly, you know. I have no idea, but I know one thing, that I knew him. All I-- when I saw him, I said good morning and good evening, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else in that first 60 would you remember that were important?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was important except him, but I knew a lot of people who were there working on the factory. He was the only one who worked in the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone by the name of Pemper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pemper. Pemper was in, in ghetto. He wasn't in Emalia from the beginning. He came after, when the Schindler list was made.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't with the first 60 in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How well did you know Pemper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pemper, I know from before the war, because Pemper's brother used to go with me to school, to the Polish school, and he could not-- he was very bad in geography. And when I was sitting in the first row, the teacher called him, show me where France is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked up in, in where Scandinavia is. So I, I said to him, down, down, down, and [LAUGHS] eventually he caught France. He saw on there written, France, and he caught it, and we used to be good friends, you know. That sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And through that, he put a good word to his brother when the list was made in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your friend Pemper's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mietek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your school friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mietek. I don't know, was it Mietek, or-- I can't remember. One of them was [? Ignatz ?] Pemper, one was Mietek Pemper.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you said, to his brother, he put in a good word for you? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because when, when I was in Plaszow, when I knew that it was going to-- they need 1,000 people for the, for the list. So I said, put a good word for me. He said that I should make sure that I'd been on list. And I didn't even rely on him. I went direct to his brother, and he knew me, too, and he said to me, I will be on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm trying to understand, why was his brother important? What role did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: His brother was the secretary of Amon Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth? He was the commander of the Plaszow concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. That's what I wanted to understand. So he was secretary to probably the most important person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the most important person. Bankier was the most important. There were quite a few important persons there.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, but in terms of authority, German authority, this guy, Amon Goeth, really controlled the Plaszow camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, he could have said to Pemper, I don't want you anymore, and got rid of Pemper.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He had him because, because he-- Pemper, he, he was a very skilled man. I mean, nobody could do what-- he could talk to you, like you do, and at the same time writing on the machine, and, and no make mistakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what I'm saying, what I understand about this person, Amon Goeth, he had the position of life or death over people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if he decided, one day, he didn't want Pemper--

speaker: interviewee
text: He could have done it. But there could be there were some monkey business behind it, which we never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So let's just go back again. So you arrived in Emalia around October '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Emalia until the time where I had to go to Plaszow. Until, I think, the end of '44. Not quite end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Emalia. You were there for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there-- as soon as I went to Emalia-- first, we went from-- as you know-- from Emalia to, to ghetto every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after, before the liquidation of the ghetto, we went from, from Plaszow to Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when were you taken to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that was-- I was taken before the liquidation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would have been January '43, February '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be-- could be something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if we say the liquidation was March '43, and you went shortly before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So January, February '43. Would that be right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, I can't remember how long, but we-- but as soon, but as soon as Schindler saw that it's going to be difficult to bring us, we saw all the atrocities, and we didn't know what's going to happen. And he managed to build the barracks on Emalia, and then we came to Emalia, and we stay on Emalia. And that could be one and a half year.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but again, would you have been in Plaszow one month, six months, 10 months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. Could be, maybe, three months. But it doesn't take long to build wooden barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I'm just trying to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get the chronology correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, you went to Plaszow from the ghetto in January, February, '43. You were there about three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't have to be exact.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just trying to understand. And then you were taken to this newly created barracks in the Emalia factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Behind the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this meant that the workers in the factory were now living on the site of the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. They were still surrounded by the, by the Lithuanian guards, which they change every so often, from Plaszow. But they had no right, ever, to come interfere with us in, in the factory. They couldn't come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? How was that arranged?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was arranged through, through Schindler. Schindler makes that arrangement.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we knew it. He didn't, he did not allow anybody to interfere. The same was in Brunnlitz. He did not let interfere with any of [INAUDIBLE] in his factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- the German used to come to make, sometime, inspection from Plaszow, which is the hierarchy of Germans. Like they were, [INAUDIBLE] maybe, officer, everything. But he used to take them upstairs to make, to make them drunk before they went down. He used to offer some cognac and everything, so that they went back and they say, everything is OK. But we were protected. We didn't work under the German guards. We were not-- we had no contact with the German guards. Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now what I'm trying to understand, when you started working in Emalia factory, there was just 60 of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that, at that stage, Schindler was looking at it as a benevolent act to help Jews, or was he more concerned with just the factory being a money-making operation? This is when you were there at the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That, I don't-- that, I don't know. He's, he's-- I couldn't pick his brain, but I know one thing, that he did not employ any more Polish workers, which he had to pay them, and he employed the Jews. And as the factory grew, he took more Jews from, from Plaszow. That's what I remember, because sometimes used to come new people. And as far-- I don't think it was benevolent, because he made money out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was purely commercial, at that start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Purely commercial. He didn't-- he got paid for us, but he didn't give us any money. He just feed us, you know. Feed us, and he kept us in comfort. I should say that he did-- that he was entitled to that. My opinion is, he's entitled to that money, because he, he protect us. Put it this way. Where everybody was starving and, and, and, and dying in Plaszow, we were sitting over there, and, and we live like, like nothing, like a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But he was also able to make himself--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was able-- He was able to make himself money on the side, and he used to come-- there was strict guidelines. The factory had to go. He was working seven days a week, day and night, working day and night, or shift, and, and he was producing the pots and pans for the military and for the other things, and he was living very well. He used to come with a-- with a Mercedes car every day dressed in different suit, and sometimes three times a day. And he had girlfriends, you know. He had a good life. But we didn't have a bad life, either, because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But when would you say, if it, if it did happen, his attitude changed, if, at all, it changed, to that of being more concerned about making money and pots and pans for the German army, to maybe trying to get more Jews out of the-- out of Plaszow and other areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he change it after he saw what's happening in the, in the Cracow ghetto, and, and maybe-- he was a very shrewd man-- and maybe he saw as a ticket to live after the war, because he knew it. Because that was the time when Stalingrad fell, and we got the news. Stalingrad fell, and Odessa fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew that the German are going bad, so he want to create a good will. So that's what he did. This is my opinion. You're saying there might have been some ulterior motives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulterior motives, but at the same time, he, he was not bad, and he had-- and he was always-- and he, he was looking after Bankier. I still say Bankier was the most important man for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going to Plaszow, you're there three months-- give or take three months--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: --what do you remember about Plaszow, the conditions in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible. I mean, I, I wasn't there daytime, so I did not experience all this. But each time we came at night, we had this happen, that happen. Somebody was killed. Somebody was hanged. And, and you hear all the time the same thing, all the atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we did-- it was not very pleasant. We're lucky that we came alive. We're lucky that we come out alive. And that was-- that's where Schindler probably made a, made a, a-- what you call it-- commitment to build, to build this place on the-- At the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the factory. And also, at the same thing-- because Bankier was coming with us, too. He didn't know what could happen to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Plaszow, you were still working in the Emalia factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, what happened was you changed, overnight, place where you slept at night, from the Cracow ghetto to the Plaszow ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: but you're still working during the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: In factory. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know, at any time, if the factory closed down before liquidation of Cracow, or during the liquidation of Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my memory. Is that what happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not to my-- maybe when I was in Plaszow, but we were never in Plaszow-- I was never in Plaszow at daytime. I was always working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daytime, I was always working at Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because before, before, you said that, as far as you're aware, the factory worked seven days a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Day and night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's some indication from other people, I think, in the Schindler book-- the film, that there was an implication that Schindler had to close the factory for a period of time during the liquidation. And I'm trying to ask you if you recall that happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't recall that. I was all the time in Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never had any day off. But it wasn't so bad, because we didn't work-- I didn't work so hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not asking if you worked hard or not. [LAUGHTER] I'm asking if you remember or recall the factory, physically, not working for any period of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't remember it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only time which I remember the factory was maybe closed, and we were in Plaszow, at the time where he had to transfer all the machinery from Plaszow into Brunnlitz. At that time, we were staying in the, in the Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're not doing very much over there, and, and that's what-- the Schindler List was born there. And that's what was-- we knew that he come one day, and he will make a list and take his people to the Brunnlitz, the people to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he caught me one day sleeping. It was the same, the same as with this policeman. He caught me one day sleeping on the job, and so he also, he warned me that, if he catch me again, I'll lose the job. That's all he did. He didn't, he didn't, he did not do me anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how would you describe him, his looks, his, his stature?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tall fellow, not bad looking. I would say he would be in his 50s, 40, 45. He wouldn't be more. All the time smoking, always cigarette in his hand. He was a chain smoker. Nicely dressed, with perfume all over his place, and that's it. That's how he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he mix a lot with the workers, go down to the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he used to-- when he went on the inspection, he always talk to the Polish foreman. He always talk to the Polish foreman. And then, when, when they work at nighttime, they had an idea. As soon as the car drive, drove-- because the, the gate wasn't very far from the main factory. The factory was on the right-hand side, and the gate was out. So as soon as they hear gate, they knew Schindler's coming, because they start to, you know, to make, make themselves busy, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- you said Polish foreman. You're saying now that the factory, even though there were 60 or more Jewish workers, there were Poles that were in charge of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The Poles were there before we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there weren't Jews in charge of Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only the Poles. They were quite nice people. They were not, they were not bad. The foreman, as long as you did the job, they knew that we are here because of this business. So they treat us-- as long as you have, you have, you have to do the job. And if, if you did the job, they didn't do you any harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the barracks were built at Emalia factory, did the Poles live there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They still went home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They still went-- the Poles always went home. And I don't know whether they work on Saturday. I can't remember it, but I, I don't think they worked seven days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any specific incident that you recall, while you were in Emalia factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Specific incident. The one specific incident is one of, of the people who were stupid, a fellow from, from Emalia, he run away. Because, from Emalia, you could easily run away. There was no problem to, to disappear, but nobody wanted. Because where would you go? You haven't got where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he run away, and Schindler had quite a bit of trouble to explain it. And the German came, and they come to the gate, and they give us another warning that if something happens, they will decimate us. You know what decimating is? Every 10th is going to be killed. So we had to be careful and make sure that nobody runs away. It was the only incident. But otherwise, nobody in their right mind would run away from Emalia. Because we knew what was going outside. We, we had papers. We had news, and the Poles used to come, so we knew that, that, that we live in a paradise. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. We don't touch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape two of the interview with John Armer. It's the 29th of June, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
